title: 2026/03/26 - JU [Part 1]
displayTitle: 2026-03-26 - Justice Committee - Review briefing on the 2024/25 Annual review of the Independent Police Conduct Authority [Part 1]
topic: Review briefing on the 2024/25 Annual review of the Independent Police Conduct Authority
description: Thursday, 26 March 2026 | Justice Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 Annual review of the Independent Police Conduct Authority Hearing of Evidence Independent Police Conduct Authority Judge Kenneth Johnston KC, Chair Peter Clinton, Manager, Corporate
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2024/25 Annual review of the Independent Police Conduct Authority. Independent Police Conduct Authority. Judge Kenneth Johnston KC, Chair

chunks.text: Please sit down. Thank you. Morning. Morning, everyone. Morning. Morning. Well, good morning, Your Honor, and both of you. It'd be great. We've got 45 minutes with you, so you've probably done this before, but very keen to maybe get some introductory comments and then open it up for questions as much as possible.

Well, I haven't done it before, is the answer. Oh, you haven't? And- Well, welcome... I've done similar things before. I'm sure you have stood in a similar situation before. And I want to start by thanking you all for the invitation. It's a great pleasure to be here. We've been looking forward to it for some time. Can I just introduce Peter Clinton, who's our corporate manager at the IPCA, who's joined me so that I don't make a pig's ear of the financial side of things?

I'm told, or I understand, that I've got three minutes. I hope- No, no, you can take longer than that. We've got 45 with me. Right. It'd be good if you gave us... I was going to say, thank you, Mr. Chairman. I was going to say that I might just exceed that- Yeah... in my verbose way, so you'll forgive me.

There are really two parts to what I want to talk to the committee about today. One is, the major thing is how we've been tracking over recent times, and so on and so forth. The second is I want to touch briefly on our expectations about the intended establishment of the Office of Inspector General of Police.

But that'll be fairly short because my knowledge about that at the moment is relatively limited. So starting with our recent work, I think the last time that the authority was before this committee was in February 2023. A great deal has changed since then, apart from the change of chair. There's also been a change in the composition of our board, and in our organization's structure, and indeed in our complement, which has shrunk for reasons I'll come to. The only thing, in fact, that has remained

constant since that time is our baseline funding, which has remained static since 2022-'23 at, you're probably already aware, $6.7 million or thereabouts. And about 90% of that is expended in personnel and rent. A whisker over 90%, I think.

And yet, since that financial year, there has been a material increase, which I want the committee to understand, in terms of both the quantity and quality and complexity of the complaints that we receive. We're primarily a complaints body, as you'll realize.

So 2022-'23, we had an 8% increase, thereabouts. These are rough figures. In 2023-'24, we had a 6% increase in complaints. This is gross numbers, of course. 9% in 2024-'25, and I confidently anticipate something north of 5% this year, which if I add those up, I come to a total of a 28% increase, and that's ignoring the compounding factor, which I'm afraid is beyond my arithmetical capacity to calculate. But it's about

something well north of a quarter of a... a complaint. An increase of a bit over a quarter. So in one sense, that's been a good thing for the authority. It's forced us, really over my period of time with it, to focus our attention on efficiency and ensuring that the funding that we do have is utilized to best advantage for the members of the public who we are there to serve.

So, I want to just take a moment to talk about the responses that we've put in place to cope with those things. So first and foremost, we've made sure over that period of time, and this has not been especially easy, to bring ourselves to a point where last year we operated within it. Yes, last year we operated within our budget.

That's not always been the case for the authority. There were some difficulties associated with the parliamentary protest and other things. But in any event, there was expenditure beyond the budget for some time, which is dangerous of course, although we have kept a responsible level of reserves.

But last year we operated within our budget. This year, I'm afraid we will be running a small deficit budget, and if we project out with fairly conservative cost increases over a period of time, which may of course become irrelevant now, that gets pretty ugly within two or three years.

So, the second thing is we've focused carefully on our management group because we wanted to ensure that theFunding that we do have is primarily directed at doing our core role rather than anything else. For example, when our general manager retired 18 months, is it 18 months somewhere there?

Mm, little over. Yeah. Ago, we didn't replace him, which I have to say, resulted in substantially increased expectations, primarily for Peter as the corporate manager, but also for me as the chair and the operation managers. Peter's borne the brunt of that, though.
And we also reduced our numbers. We had some folk there who had been there for a while and,

in any event, we ended up with a tighter management group. So the third thing that we did was a modest reorganization to the way in which we operated, so that our three operational managers in particular, were able to focus on the three areas of activity, which is the resolution section dealing with the bulk of complaints. The section which focuses on supervision of police inquiries and the section which focuses on independent investigations. So that was not as clear as it was. We focused on that and it's worked very well.

And, the fourth thing, and this has been very important for us, we have developed strategies to deal with what you may think I call unkindly, meritless complaints. You can imagine that we get a number of complaints which obviously from day one are not going anywhere.

Let me just give the committee an example so that you know the sort of thing I'm talking about. Someone gets caught or pulled over for doing 135K on Desert Road and he or she complains that they were given a ticket when it was a very clear day and there was no other traffic around and that the policeman was a little rude to her about her driving or his driving or whatever. And really what they want is the ticket to be...

They think that we have some power to dispense with the ticket. We don't. And we need to tell them that quickly before we spend time. In the past, we have carried out sort of minor investigations and written long involved letters. One of the things I've done is implement what we call a summary process, which you probably can see immediately, especially those of you who have a legal background, reflects what happens in the high court.

And those things are dealt with pretty swiftly by a small group of people who are discerning, and we try to be very careful to ensure that nothing slips through the net that ought to be looked at more carefully. So that's enabled us to deal with some things efficiently, I think. And then finally, the fifth thing we've done is try and be more discerning about what we call the thematic work that we do.

Let me give you an example of that as well. The Free Speech Union, which is vocal and an important party in this sort of area, advanced three specific complaints about the way police dealt recently with some cases involving mental health issues.

And invited us not only to look at the three particular complaints, but also to do a thematic review of the police's policy in this area. In the past, we might have done that. We just don't have the resources to do it. So we've gone back and received some criticism for this.

We've said, "Of course, we will look at the complaints. They look serious and important, but we just can't engage in a long involved thematic review." Now, can I just say, this is a matter of concern to me, because I think that one of the most effective areas that the IPCA-- or one of the most effective things it's done over the years is to engage in that thematic work. So it's a matter of disappointment to me that I can't allow the staff to do more of that sort of work. But there we go.

That's just basically a resource issue. I'm sure everyone understands. So, I want to say, having described the things that we've done, I want to say that in my judgment, we have gone just about as far as we possibly can down these roads of efficiency.

And any further down that path would present a serious risk of not being able to discharge our statutory responsibilities. We would be at risk of turning away complaints which really do deserve a careful examination. And frankly, I have to say that without-... even a modest increase in our budget, and I know that the committee's not necessarily looking at this, but I can't contain myself. I feel I've got to say this.

Without at least a modest increase in our budget from the 1st of July, I think we are already at that cusp, at that risk area. We've got a bid in, so we'll see what happens, but I just wanted the committee to know, get a real flavor for where we are with some of these things.Acknowledging, of course, the constraints that the government and the public purse is under.

Yeah. So let me give you some highlights and get on to cheerier topic and give you some highlights about the last three or so years. We have managed to deal with-- Am I running out of time? No, just a couple more. Five more minutes. Seven. Easily.

Okay. We've dealt with all of our complaints. We've completed some very large and complex investigations. We've dealt with the individual complaints as opposed to the general complaint arising out of parliamentary protest. We've had the Allen Hall wrongful conviction

investigation, which was the subject of prosecution in the house. So I don't want to say any more about that. We've had the Farzana Yaqoob case, which was really important. A young Muslim woman who was murdered by someone who was stalking her and our report, I think, helped to highlight some of the inadequacies in the way our legislation reads and the way it's enforced.

So that was important. You might have heard about this one, the McSkimming investigation of last year. And we're currently engaged in a long and difficult investigation involving the Fuller Sands wrongful convictions of the murder several years ago.

So we've done a lot of very heavy lifting, I think, in that period of time. By no means, not all by me, in fact, far from it. We've got a group of fantastic people. So, despite what I said about thematic reviews, we've also managed to fit in a long and difficult thematic review involving policing protests and also, the CEPOL, I forget what the acronym stands for, but the interviewing technique, the sort of friendly sitting in a couch with people and interviewing technique.

And during that time we've maintained, I think, a stable and dedicated workforce, which I'm very proud of. And I like to think that we've had a genuine impact on the police and how they operate, and through that, a positive impact on the way the New Zealand public are served by the police.

By no means perfect, but I think we've had a positive impact. And we can talk about, if you wish, the measures that we use to try and analyze that, but I'd just prefer, I'm likely to end up referring back to the Yaqoob and McSkimming cases. Anyway, that's all I wanted to say about our recent work. Can I just spend less than a minute on the future?

Yes. Assuming it looks likely that the proposal to create an office and role of Inspector General of Police proceeds, the authority, in its current form at least, doesn't have an independent future. The new entity will have an own motion power, which, frankly, my predecessor and I have been pressing for for years.

And a much wider jurisdiction comparable to the jurisdiction of the officers dealing with the spy and defense organizations. So our core complaints-based function, which neither of those organizations have, will remain. But as I understand it at the moment, none of that is going to be in place until towards the end of next year, which means my retirement is postponed.

But that's as may be. To date, the senior management team at the IPC have been working very closely with the ministry and with police to try and help identify the difficulties associated with that, and that's proceeding apace. But I'm unable to say what the new organization might look like.

All I've done in that regard is to give my staff assurance that I'll hang around and help them through that process until it winds its way to a conclusion, as these things do, in the fullness of time, as they say. So I'm sorry, I'll probably take a lot more- Your golf handicap will take a battering, I think. Well, my golf handicap is zero, but my hunting handicap's not too bad.

All right. Okay. Hey, now I've got a whole lot of people lining up for questions. So the first one's Mr. Todd Stevenson. Oh, thank you. Well, thank you both for being here. And thanks for outlining basically the over 25% increase in complaints you're receiving and some of the issues. That's great. Have you been able to pick up any kind of themes through that increase in complaints? Is there anything that kind of, yeah, any commonality? I'm just curious. Yeah.

You mean of the additional ones? Well, of the additional or you might be just getting more complaints in a particular area. Anything that springs to mind with that. Yeah. It fluctuates. Let me try. I think by and large, if you stood back and looked at the stats, and I'm going to use your question as an opportunity to talk about one other tiny thing in a moment, but because it's a good question.

If you stood back and look at it, you would probably not see much of a different pattern. Right. It's probably just more-... generally over the scope of things. But there are changes from time to time. For example, we had a terrible run a couple of years ago of dog bites.Police seem to be using dogs in a fairly vigorous way, and a lot of time and effort was put in. We dealt with a lot of complaints.

That seems to have fallen off. So there's a change. I hope, I'd like to think because of the work that the IPCA put in with police, because police are extraordinarily cooperative in all of these areas. And I dare say there would be some opportunity to look at patterns, but here's my dig. We haven't got the resources to engage either internally by employment or externally by data analytics.

Right. We've been trying to do that for about five years, because that is an avenue to identify when influence things most effectively. But we just can't afford it. Right. Okay, thanks. I've got Ms. Camellia Bellet. Yeah. Thanks very much. It was really interesting to hear about the work that you do. I just had some questions around the responses to written questions that you put forward.

Yes. And so I'll just take- Should I-... kind of in two different areas. Should I get out my version of the- Yeah... written? Okay. You could. The first one was just in... You may not need to read it exactly, but in the answer to question 54, you talked about two different protected disclosures that you had received as an organization. The second one is clearly in relation to police conduct. The first one I just wanted to know, and obviously there may be some restrictions or sensitivities about what you can tell us about in a public hearing. But with that in mind, I just

wanted to ask whether the first matter was something relating to a complaint that you'd received about police conduct, and if there was anything else you could tell us about that matter. And the second area that I was interested in was question seven, where you talk about the fact that over the last five years, the authority hasn't had a gender pay gap.

That's quite an unusual answer for us to see in responses to questions. And I just wanted to ask how you went about calculating that. I can see from the outline of senior staff that there is a-- It might be that they are different people despite their gender are paid the same amount of money, but I just wanted to know how you calculated that.

Okay. It's a bit unusual for us to see that. Yeah. It's strange you should ask that, because when I read the first draft of these responses-... I wondered about that very question, and I'll come to it. Let me deal with this first. I don't have a specific recollection of these protected disclosures, but bullying corruption, I believe I know what that involves. And it is a section, I don't think there's anything troubling about me saying it, because it's almost obvious anyway.

It's a section within police where there was a bit of factionalization and complaints probably on both sides about bullying by senior staff. One of the interesting sort of demarcation points for us is we don't see ourselves as the police human rights, sorry, not human rights, human resources department.

And we often when something of that sort comes in say, "Well, that's something that you've got to sort out." But when there is an obvious allegation of particularly bad behavior or inappropriate behavior on behalf of an identified officer or identified officers, we will look at it, and that would've been that event. Where in the country that was, I'm sorry, I don't know.

As-- You don't know? No, sorry. As to the first question, I think our answer, in fact, I'm certain that our answer is formulated on what you may well think is an old-fashioned view of the gender pay gap, which is it doesn't matter who's in the role, we are paid the same amount, whether it's male or female.

And I know that there's a much more sophisticated way of looking at that-... or a very different way of looking at that. And I don't think we have gone through a complex assessment of that nature. So I think that's a simplistic answer to the question. We simply have a role. It's paid X dollars.

It doesn't matter whether it's male or female. And that may not be the most adequate sort of view or approach to it in other people's minds, but I'm afraid that that's what we've done. Is that fair? That's fair. And, sorry, I might just add that we are right now going through an external review of our remuneration, and that will result in a refresh, perhaps, of our remuneration bands, obviously market rates. And also we'll move from there to a remuneration policy as well. So an updated policy.

So that's an external party that's providing support for that. It's been three years since we've done that exercise, so we think that that's timely that we do it now. So we will actually have an updated, modern, comprehensive strategy and policy at that point. Right. Yeah, no, I appreciate the answer, and I think that's what I had garnered by reading your answer.

What we usually are looking for when we ask, and these are questions that get asked to every agency, is a kind of calculation regardless of the band. So if that was work that was undertaken during your remuneration review, I'm sure the committee would be interested to see if there was any updated statistics.

We can certainly provide that. All right, cool. Now in terms of Paul Thank you. Mōrena. So I've got a couple questions, so would appreciate your brevity in answer, if that's okay. Sorry, not trying to be rude, it's just we don't have a lot of time left. The government introduced the Policing Amendment Bill this week, which is a response to an IPCA report which found that the collection of tens of thousands of images of people was unlawful, and it was also a report by the privacy commissioner. They're now changing the law to make that lawful.

Once you've done your report, do you follow what happens after that, or you leave your report and that's that? No. So are you concerned that that's setting a precedent whereby the IPCA finds the police to be doing something unlawful, and then the government comes in and legalizes those actions?

You're inviting me to have a political view about that. That's okay if you can't answer. That's all good. I don't. Yep. That's fine. So, the government have introduced a number of laws that rely on the police making calls and judgments. The ones that I'm thinking about are the gang patch ban, and I want to understand the interaction between the police and the courts and the IPCA because people who have had their property seized, has been ordered to be returned by the courts.

Mm-hmm. And I'm wondering whether there's this disconnect between the government and the judiciary and whether that is also leading to further complaints or could be contributing to a higher volume of complaints. Sure. Again, I'm not sure that's something I am in a position to comment on.

You know how the system works as well as I do, which is the government passes the legislation, the courts interpret and apply it- Mm... as best they can. There may be tensions for all I know about those things, but not that I'm particularly to stay with. That's fine. I'm just wondering if there's a connection between the attorney general making a report before a bill is passed- Mm-hmm... that warns that there are things that might breach human rights, and then it is enacted and then it is enforced.

Yep. And then it is, could that contribute to a higher volume of complaints, is what I'm saying? No. Potentially? Yes. Okay. And then the other question I had is around resourcing. So I know you said in your opening remarks that you don't have a whole lot of details to talk about in terms of the Inspector General of Police.

But are the IPCA thinking about whether your resources will be reduced in order to resource that new office, or are you hoping that yours will stay the same? I can talk to that. Yep. My understanding of the position, and I'm very confident about this because I've been closely connected with the ministers involved and the Justice Department and the police, all of whom have been enormously helpful.

What will emerge, I am confident, is a new organization, which will be headed by the Inspector General of Police, and that will incorporate the IPCA in its, essentially in its current form, the complaints component, but it will have other things attached to it.

Okay. So do you- So I'm not concerned that there's going to be any- Loss of your resources in order to resource that office. No, because it'll be one in- Yeah. Our resource will be- Subsumed. Is that the right- Subsumed is a good word. Mm. Okay, so you're not worried about there being too much crossing over and stepping on toes?

No. No, I can say with peace it's subsumed. There's going to be one organization. Okay, now let's go to the honorable Dr. Duncan Webb. Kia ora, Judge. Look, kind of a couple of themed questions. And if I can introduce it by saying I think your organization's about trust in the wider justice network, but specifically in policing. And so with that in mind, I'd be interested in knowing a little bit more about your summary disposal framework, and just how we can reassure people who make complaints, which are summarily disposed of-

Mm-hmm that it's a fair and rigorous process. Yeah. I've got a good answer to that, and it is- It's not a trick question. No, I know. I know it's not. It's a very fair and useful question, and I'm pleased you've raised it. Just to let you know exactly how it works, we've got two specialist people who read the complaints, and identify whether they can see any merit in them. There are some which you'll appreciate from sovereign citizens or whatever, which are obviously, don't need to go any further.

There are some which, although they may have been filtered through that particular sieve, obviously needed to because there's some obvious merit in them. And then there's a group in the middle. That group in the middle is probably 25%, a quarter. They are referred to our resolutions manager, Cath Annan, or her team leader, Sarah Rolfe, and they have another look at it.And the reason I can be confident that people aren't sitting simmering because they haven't been heard is they do get a response and an explanation, and the return

percentage from those is minuscule. It really is de minimis compared with the next layer, which are the people that we look at in a reasonably short form of investigation and give them a full response, which is usually quite high because they'll come back and argue about the merits of it. But the summary folk Just to clarify, the summary ones, are they all declined?

For a summary disposal, does there have to be a declined result? Well, if it's not declined, it's passed on to the next stage and it's not treated as summary anymore. Okay. Yeah, I know that. Well, that answers that question. Can I then go to my next part of that question, which is around your category B complaints- Mm-hmm... which is about 200, I think. And these are ones which I think raise the question of independence most acutely, right? Because these are legitimate complaints about the police that the police then investigate-

Yes... on your behalf and under- Yes... your supervision. Yes. So once again, I guess the question is ultimately the same. How can someone with a category B complaint be confident that the I bit of the IPCA is real? Mm-hmm. Another perfectly fair question.

And I was not intending to be at all facetious in response. If I had my way, we'd be resourced to investigate them all independently, but we just can't. There's, I mean, at a rate of five and a half thousand complaints a year- Mm... we'd need to be at least four times the size we are to do that.

But what we assure people, and I assure the committee, is that we have a very vigorous review process. The police themselves are very good at this. No one connected with it is involved in investigating. They usually almost always take it out of the district, and then they report to us on the way through.

They are obliged to carry out whatever investigations we direct them to carry out in the course of looking at the complaint. And we will do an independent letter to the complainant saying, "Here's the level of supervision we provided here. Here's why we were dissatisfied with this component and told them to do a bit more.

Here's the views we have." To go back to your first comment, though, because it wasn't facetious at all. Ideally, there's still government resources going into it, it's just coming from a different department, right? So ideally, you would have a team of people. Why do you think would be better to have the resources to investigate those- Well-... 200 yourself?

For the very reason that underlies your question in the first place, which is that it would be absolutely a guarantee of independence- That's right of a stronger sort than exists at the moment. But Are there incidents where, in these category of cases, your independent review shows that the investigation is either not up to scratch or infected in some way with a bias?

Certainly there are. And one of the things that we have been pressing very hard for, and I think successfully, is that the new organization, when it emerges, can direct the police to investigate matters which they're not investigating at the moment and so on and so forth.

And when we disagree, we say so. And I mean, I'll give you an example. About two years ago, a senior police officer who it transpired was hitting his, or had hit his kids with a belt. The police investigated. They decided that there was nothing serious enough to warrant prosecution or anything else.

We disagreed, and I said so publicly. So- I think I said that I couldn't understand the conclusion they'd reached. Back to those class B cases. If there's a defect in the investigation, are the police obliged to follow your direction in terms of, say they didn't interview someone.

No. And you say, "You should have interviewed this possible witness. Go and do it." No. The police can turn around and say, "We'd rather not, then"? No. Once an investigation starts, we can direct them to carry out- Certainly. Yeah... pursue it in various ways. A very interesting exchange. Right. I've got Rima Nakhaie.

Oh, yes. Thank you. Thank you, Your Honor. I'd like to ask, if I may, you mentioned that you wish you could conduct more thematic research. If you were able to in an ideal world, what would be maybe three themes that you'd- Oh, dear... get onto straight away?

Oh, thank you. Well, I think one of the things that we'd dearly like to do is to delve-- I can only think of one. Yeah. Dealing with a very limited mind here, I'm afraid. No. But the one that immediately occurs to me is we had actually somebody from the United States from theLos Angeles Police Department, or the Los Angeles equivalent of us, which happens to be part of the police in Los Angeles, who spent three months with us last year and did some really outstanding research and set out how we could look at discrimination and police bias and this, that, and the next thing, and a

whole lot of areas. We, for years, have wanted to get into that area, and we just can't. So that's my best example. Okay. Can I ask another question, please, Mr. Chair? With respect to the Inspector General of Police, what planning's taken place by the authority in response to this imminent placement, and how would that look in practice?

Well, it's not as imminent as it was. It's been pushed back a bit, so I think October 2027 is the sort of kickoff date. Clearly, things will have to be done before then. Yes. But it's not really for us to be planning that. That will be largely politically directed and through the Justice Department, and we will engage and do what we need to do to put ourselves in a position where whoever's appointed, which won't be me, I have to say, hit the ground running in October next year.

Okay, so at this stage, it's more a case of when it happens- Yeah... you'll respond accordingly. I'm a child when it comes to political matters. But I don't think a Cabinet paper's even gone up yet. Yep. Okay. Thank you. All right. We've got Mr. Tom Rutherford.

Yeah. I want to talk about that case where a police officer had a relationship with a gang member- Mm... and then subsequently was leaking information from the police database to that associate. Can you talk about your role in that case? Sure.

And what was undertaken? Yep. Can I broaden it? Yep. It might be helpful if I broaden it a bit. Police have a database called NIAS, which I think it stands for National Information something or other. And for a long time we have been very concerned about the discipline around searching NIAS.

Obviously the purpose is you see a car speeding, you look up the registration. And we discovered as a result of the odd complaint for a period of time, that certain police officers, this wasn't everybody, of course, but certain police officers were habitually using it for sort of personal reasons.

Mm-hmm. Looking up their brother-in-law or something like that. And we went on a bit of a campaign and, after a period of time, the police, who I keep emphasizing, enormously cooperative in dealing with us, they're much stricter about that now. So they monitor it much more carefully.

They ensure that, they can't look at everyone, but I guess they dip sample- Yep... and so forth. And they try and exercise a degree of discipline over that. If you're a nurse and you look up the computer, I know this from chairing the Medical Discipline Tribunal, a doctor or a nurse who looks up the hospital computer for their own reasons, they lose their practicing certificate.

Mm-hmm. And the police are now taking this very seriously. What about, close to that, the situation we were made aware of last year where 30,000 breath alcohol tests- Yes... were falsified by police officers. What would your role be in monitoring or enforcement in that space?

Well, this sort of gets back to Mr. Webb's question about category Bs. We treated that as a category B because the police took that enormously seriously. They acknowledged from day one that this was completely unacceptable, and they created a little team to decide how to deal with it as best they could.

And so we monitored what they were doing, and we agreed with the strategies that they were putting in place to examine what had happened and discipline people where appropriate and so forth, and it's still going on. What about the many thousand police officers we have across the country who ultimately turn up day in, day out to serve their communities to keep- Mm-hmm... people safe. For the most part, all of them do a fantastic job. Would that be your assessment?

And there is- It's my- Just like in any organization, there is the occasional bad egg. There's some in this place. Good heavens. You astound me. But- Not on this committee, though. No. But for the most part, from your perspective and the work you've overseen- Yeah... is that the assessment you would agree with? Well, absolutely. Our police are astonishing. They work long hours.

They put themselves in danger every day. I couldn't do it. And I am just full of admiration. But, as you say, like every organization, there are people who just don't behave appropriately. Can I just ask an extension around the data? We're hearing on the Arms Bill right now, a whole lot of submissions, a lot of concerns about data breaches or possible data breaches, obviously-... information we knew about the Palmerston North situation.

Does that come under your jurisdiction? No. It would if there was a suggestion of impropriety, and in fact, we have done quite a lot of work around theCurrent arms- Registration... uh, organization. And, that's again, that's bullying and so forth in that area. No, but with the registered data, the concerns around, search your records, you're a hunter.

I don't know what type of hunter, but you may have a whole lot of, uh, rifles at your home that has, uh, leaked, and therefore you could be a burglary, um, obviously. Yeah. Well, from a personal point of view, that has concerned me when I had to re-register my firearms. And I have nothing like 100, I have to say, but, um- But, does that come under your jurisdiction?

Well it would if there was impropriety-... if there was a suggestion of a leak or something like that- Cool... and somebody had been- Right... contacting gang members or... Right. We're going to do two questions, one from you, uh, from Camilla, and then we're going to do one from Tempest. Thank you. I had a question around, um, decision decisions by police. Sometimes we get correspondence- Right... um, around decisions that the police have to, whether they've decided to prosecute or not. And obviously as legislators, that's not something

we can be- Mm-hmm... involved in at all. Mm-hmm. Like it would not be appropriate for us. And it's the people that contact us are not satisfied with that- Yes... um, that response. Is that something that you... I know that you've looked at, obviously looked at instances of where prosecutions are taken and people have, um, complaints about that. But when there's, say, a clear law that the police aren't utilizing- Mm-hmm... is that something that you would, um, have capacity to look into?

Mr. Chairman, can I have another three quarters of an hour? No. Uh, you've touched on a very enormous concern of mine. Let me try and put this briefly. In the England and Wales, the Crown Prosecution Service make decisions about that. In, uh, Scotland, the Procurator Fiscal Service makes decisions about that. In Northern Ireland, they have their own Crown Prosecution Service.

In Eyre, they have their own prosecution service. In every state of the United States, the district attorneys, uh, have to sign off on that. In every state in Australia, and on a federal basis, there are a, a corresponding organizations that have to deal with that. In every province in Canada. We are the standout exception.

The, um, uh, conflict or the potential conflict that the public is entitled to be worried about is blindingly obvious. I have been on a bit of a campaign and, um, you'll be pleased to hear that I think I have persuaded... Well, I've certainly persuaded the commissioner, uh, who agrees entirely.

Um, uh, and I think that when the new legislation emerges, part of it will be an amendment in that area which brings a degree of genuine independence, I hope, to the prosecution of police officers. Um, at least for criminal as opposed to summary offenses. All right. We're now out of time. Do you really need a question or not?

Yep. It could be a yes or no. Right. Ah. Um, just- Go for it. No, because I'm really passionate about, um, stalking and women's experience of stalking. Right. Um, thank you for your work around what happened to Farzana Yaqub. Did you feel that following the investigation that, um, because we haven't seen the report because it wasn't released publicly-... but we did know what the recommendations were.

Did you feel like those were translated into the stalking law that eventuated last year with unanimous support or more- Y- yes, yes, we did. Did you feel- We did. Yeah. Were you able to inform the development of that legislation? Uh, we had a mild hand in it, but we didn't have to do very much because- Cool... um, I think it was cross-party support for it.

Yeah. You know, for toughening up- All right. He, I want to thank you, uh, for your service. Keep going until 27. Man. Keep the message- There's gotta, you've gotta be more, there's a bit of rhyme there somewhere, isn't there? Thank you for coming today out. It's been a fascinating-... conversation. Thank you. so you... Right. Miss? Feel I should bow. No. No. Don't do that, no.
